nodes.title: Regulate AI like the Fed regulates banks
nodes.body:
Most "regulate AI" takes reach for an FDA for AI — a one-time approval gate before release. The Fed analogy points somewhere subtler: bank supervision is continuous. The regulator never stops watching.

Fed → banks
Agency → AI labs
Stress tests
Capability evals
Embedded examiners
Embedded auditors
Capital buffers
Safety thresholds
Systemic-risk watch
Frontier-risk watch
The key difference: continuous supervision, not a single gate. The Fed keeps talking to banks forever — "constantly talking and monitoring risk."

Caveat: the right-hand column is what the analogy suggests, not what exists. It's a thinking tool, not a claim.

queue.title: Goodfire — the world inside neural networks
queue.body:
Mechanistic interpretability: reading what's actually happening inside a model rather than just judging its outputs.

The bridge I keep noticing: interpretability is to a model what an examiner's audit is to a bank, or what monitoring is to any system you can't fully trust. All three are "seeing inside."

Read →

queue.title: Monitoring the EM fields of GPU / data centers
queue.body: Can you observe what compute is doing from the outside — side-channel signals, power draw, electromagnetic emissions — as a verification or governance tool? "Seeing what's going on" without needing to be let inside.

queue.title: Interest rates' relationship to AI
queue.body: Does the cost of capital shape the pace and shape of the AI buildout? Cheap money fuels speculative infrastructure; expensive money forces discipline. If compute is the new capital expenditure, rates may be an underrated lever on the whole field.
